hypothesis: Adam at K=32 failed badly, so the next operator-priority axis to probe is preprocessing rather than more optimizer churn. The strongest low-K EF=16 backbone is still bucketed_lowrank_two_stage_soft_codebook_residual at K=32; enabling Hadamard preprocessing on that controlled recipe tests whether a simple activation transform can improve the smallest exposed frontier point without changing architecture or capacity.
summary: Run a parameter-only preprocessing ablation on the K=32 bucketed backbone: keep architecture, K, EF, optimizer, LR, and AUXK fixed, and enable `USE_HADAMARD` to test whether preprocessing improves the low-K Pareto point.
self_review: This follows the operator hint to shift away from architecture invention and probes a new axis after Adam clearly underperformed. The risk is that Hadamard already failed on a routed K=16 run, but testing it on the current stronger bucketed K=32 backbone is still a clean single-variable check before considering loss-function changes.
notes_to_memory: Round 132 should pivot from optimizer to preprocessing after Adam severely regressed on the bucketed K=32 backbone.
Use the current K=32 bucketed frontier model as the preprocessing testbed because improving the smallest exposed non-dominated point is higher value than perturbing the already-strong K=128 anchor.
next_hypotheses: If Hadamard improves K=32 materially, propagate the same preprocessing test to K=64 on the bucketed family to see whether the gain generalizes across the mid-K lane.
If Hadamard is neutral or worse on bucketed K=32, treat simple preprocessing as unlikely to help this backbone and pivot next to a loss-function ablation on the same controlled recipe.
If Hadamard causes instability or severe regression again, deprioritize preprocessing and refocus on alternative loss objectives rather than further recipe churn.
